HEARING ON PLAINTIFF'S EX PARTE APPLICATION TO VACATE RULING
Off Calendar Aug-18-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. ANTHONY W. WERBIN, Esq. of Downtown L.A. Law Group (Tel: 213-389-3765) for plaintiff;
No other appearances made in this matter.

HEARING ON PLAINTIFF'S EX PARTE APPLICATION TO VACATE RULING.

The Court notes it did not receive any notice of contest, and adopts its tentative ruling as follows:

Motion denied. First, C.C.P. Sec. 473(b) does not mandate relief here, as the moving party argues. See Huh v. Wang, et al., 158 Cal. Ap. 4th 1406 (2007). Rather, C.C.P. Sec. 473(b)'s discretionary relief provision applies to this motion as the underlying order was one granting the motion to compel deposition and request for monetary sanctions. See Zamora v. Clayborn Contracting Group, Inc., 28 Cal. 4th 249 (2002); Henderson v. Pacific Gas and Electric Co.,187 Cal. App. 4th 215, 226-229 (2010); Garcia v. Hejmadi, 58 Cal. App. 4th 674, 681 (1997). Inexcusable neglect prevents relief, and an attorney's inexcusable neglect is chargeable to the client. Carroll v. Abbott Laboratories, Inc., 32 Cal. 3d 892 (1982).

This is a situation where the underlying order was made pursuant to a motion to compel that was opposed, and the opposing party's counsel failed to appear in court for the hearing. The underlying order was made after the Court had considered the moving and opposing papers, such that the order was not made due to the moving party's absence but was in fact on the merits. It is argued that counsel for the moving party did not appear because he used the wrong link to attend the virtual court hearing. It is similar to a situation where counsel was unfamiliar with the court location, made no effort in advance of the court hearing to learn about the location, then physically went to the wrong court location, resulting in counsel missing the hearing entirely. It is clear that counsel for the moving party was unprepared to attend a discovery motion hearing at the San Francisco Superior Court and made no effort to learn about how to do so until the morning of the hearing. Therefore, the Court does not find "mistake, inadvertence, surprise, or excusable neglect."

The prevailing party is required to prepare and submit a proposed order to the pro tem judge at williamclynn@gmail.com.
